1: qôlî ʾel-yhwh ʾezʿāq qôlî ʾel-yhwh ʾetḥannān — "with full voice I cry out to the LORD; with full voice I plead with him for mercy" — the doubled qôlî (my voice, my voice) — emphatic: this is audible, full-throated prayer, not silent or formal. The superscription connects this to David in a cave (1 Samuel 22:1, Adullam; or 1 Sam 24, En-gedi), giving it the setting of fugitive desperation. ʾezʿāq (I cry out, shout for help, from zāʿaq, the verb for the Israelites' cry in Egypt, Exodus 2:23) + ʾetḥannān (I plead for grace/mercy, Hithpael of ḥānan) — the two modes of desperate prayer: the urgent shout and the pleading for mercy.
2: ʾešpōk lĕpānāyw śîḥî ṣārātî lĕpānāyw ʾaggîd — "I pour out my troubles before him; I lay my distress before him" — ʾešpōk śîḥî (I pour out my complaint/meditation, from šāpak, to pour out + śîaḥ, meditation, complaint, musing) — the liquid metaphor for prayer: what has been accumulating inside is now poured out before YHWH. ṣārātî (my distress, from ṣārar, to be in a narrow place, constrained) — the claustrophobia of being hemmed in. Both śîaḥ and ṣārāh are laid "before his face" (lĕpānāyw) — the face of YHWH as the place of honest presentation.
3: bĕhiṭʿaṭṭēp ʿālay rûḥî wĕʾattāh yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî bĕʾōraḥ-zû ʾăhallēk ṭāmĕnû pah lî — "when my spirit grows faint within me, you know my path. In the path I must travel, they have hidden a trap for me" — hiṭʿaṭṭēp (to faint, languish, cover oneself, from ʿāṭap, to be faint, to cover) — the spirit collapsing over itself like someone falling unconscious. yādaʿtā nĕtîbātî (you know my path) — the Psalter's consolation: YHWH's knowledge is not merely informational but relational and protective. Even the hidden trap is known to him. ṭāmĕnû pah lî (they have hidden a trap for me) — the trap is concealed from the psalmist but not from YHWH.
4: habbeṭ yamîn ûrĕʾēh wĕʾên-lî makkîr ʾābad mānôs mimmennî ʾên dôrēš lĕnapšî — "I look to my right and see — no one acknowledges me; all escape has perished from me; no one cares for my soul" — habbeṭ yamîn ûrĕʾēh (look to the right and see) — the right side was the position of the defense advocate in court (Psalms 109:31; Psalms 110:5). The psalmist looks for a defender and finds none. makkîr (one who knows/acknowledges, from nākar) — no one recognizes him or admits knowing him. ʾābad mānôs (escape has perished/is gone) — the exit has disappeared. ʾên dôrēš lĕnapšî (no one is seeking [the welfare of] my soul) — in Hebrew, to "seek" someone's nepeš can mean either to inquire about their welfare or to seek their life; the phrase is deliberately ambiguous — no one seeks his good, and those who are seeking him are his enemies.
5: zāʿaqtî ʾēlêkā yhwh ʾāmartî ʾattāh maḥsî ḥelqî bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm — "I cry to you, O LORD; I say, 'You are my shelter and my portion in this living world'" — maḥsî (my shelter, refuge, from ḥāsāh) — YHWH as the refuge when human advocates are absent (v4). ḥelqî (my portion, my share, from ḥālaq, to divide, to allot) — land-allotment language transferred to YHWH himself: the psalmist's inheritance is not land but the God who gives land. This identification (YHWH as the Levitical portion — cf. Numbers 18:20; Psalms 73:26; Lamentations 3:24) removes the psalmist's ultimate dependence from any earthly allotment. bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm (in the land of the living) — not in Sheol's silence but in the world where YHWH acts.
6: haqšîbāh ʾel-rinnātî kî-dalôtî mĕʾōd haṣṣîlēnî mĕrōdĕpay kî ʾāmĕṣû mimmennî — "hear my cry, for I have been brought very low; rescue me from those who pursue me, for they are too strong for me" — dalôtî mĕʾōd (I am very low/reduced, Qal perfect of dālal, to hang low, be reduced, to be thin, impoverished) — complete depletion. rōdĕpay (my pursuers, from rādap, to pursue, chase, persecute) — the same verb for Saul pursuing David. ʾāmĕṣû mimmennî (they are stronger than I am) — the honest admission of being outmatched; the prayer is precisely because the psalmist cannot save himself.
7: hôṣîʾāh mimmaskēr napšî lĕhôdôt ʾet-šimĕkā bî yakkĕtrû ṣaddîqîm kî tigmōl ʿālay — "lead my soul out of this prison so I can praise your name; then the righteous will gather around me, because you will have been generous to me" — mimmaskēr (from the prison/enclosure, from sākar, to shut, enclose) — the cave of v1's superscription becomes the prison. lĕhôdôt ʾet-šimĕkā (to praise/acknowledge your name) — the motivation for rescue is worship: the dead cannot praise (Psalms 115:17), so rescue enables the very thing YHWH desires. bî yakkĕtrû ṣaddîqîm (the righteous will crown/encircle themselves around me) — kātar (to surround, encircle, crown) — community restored around the delivered one. kî tigmōl ʿālay (because you will have been generous/will have dealt fully with me) — gāmal (to deal fully, recompense, wean) — the generous divine dealing becomes the rallying point of the community.
